1,962 killed[[5]](#cite_note-Kober-5) * 1,603 killed by Israelis[[5]](#cite_note-Kober-5) * 359 killed by Palestinians[[5]](#cite_note-Kober-5)

* Palestinian protestors confront Israeli troops in [Gaza City](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City"), 1987 * Israeli military checkpoint near [Jabalia](/wiki/Jabalia "Jabalia") in the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"), 1988 | |

([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الانتفاضة الأولى, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *al-Intifāḍa al-’Ūlā*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'The First Uprising'), also known as the **First Palestinian Intifada**,[[4]](#cite_note-Nasrallah56-4)[[6]](#cite_note-Alimi2006_1-6) was a sustained uprising involving violent and [non-violent protests](/wiki/Nonviolent_resistance "Nonviolent resistance"), acts of [civil disobedience](/wiki/Civil_disobedience "Civil disobedience"), [riots](/wiki/Riot "Riot"), and [terrorist attacks](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") carried out by [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") civilians and [militants](/wiki/Palestinian_political_violence "Palestinian political violence") in the [Israeli-occupied](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") [Palestinian territories](/wiki/Palestinian_territories "Palestinian territories") and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[7]](#cite_note-King2-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Ruth_Margolies_Beitler_p._128-9) It was motivated by collective Palestinian frustration over Israel's military occupation of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") and the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") as it approached a twenty-year mark, having begun in the wake of the [1967 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War").[[10]](#cite_note-LockmanBeinin1989_5-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The uprising lasted from December 1987 until the [Madrid Conference of 1991](/wiki/Madrid_Conference_of_1991 "Madrid Conference of 1991"), though some date its conclusion to 1993, the year the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords") were signed.[[4]](#cite_note-Nasrallah56-4)

1987–1993 Palestinian uprising against Israel

This article is about the 1987–1993 Palestinian uprising against Israel. For the 1999–2004 Sahrawi uprising against Morocco, see [First Sahrawi Intifada](/wiki/First_Sahrawi_Intifada "First Sahrawi Intifada").

The **First Intifada** ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الانتفاضة الأولى, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *al-Intifāḍa al-’Ūlā*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'The First Uprising'), also known as the **First Palestinian Intifada**,[[4]](#cite_note-Nasrallah56-4)[[6]](#cite_note-Alimi2006_1-6) was a sustained uprising involving violent and [non-violent protests](/wiki/Nonviolent_resistance "Nonviolent resistance"), acts of [civil disobedience](/wiki/Civil_disobedience "Civil disobedience"), [riots](/wiki/Riot "Riot"), and [terrorist attacks](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") carried out by [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") civilians and [militants](/wiki/Palestinian_political_violence "Palestinian political violence") in the [Israeli-occupied](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") [Palestinian territories](/wiki/Palestinian_territories "Palestinian territories") and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[7]](#cite_note-King2-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Ruth_Margolies_Beitler_p._128-9) It was motivated by collective Palestinian frustration over Israel's military occupation of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") and the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") as it approached a twenty-year mark, having begun in the wake of the [1967 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War").[[10]](#cite_note-LockmanBeinin1989_5-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The uprising lasted from December 1987 until the [Madrid Conference of 1991](/wiki/Madrid_Conference_of_1991 "Madrid Conference of 1991"), though some date its conclusion to 1993, the year the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords") were signed.[[4]](#cite_note-Nasrallah56-4)

The Intifada began on 9 December 1987[[12]](#cite_note-EdSaid-12) in the [Jabalia refugee camp](/wiki/Jabalia_refugee_camp "Jabalia refugee camp") after an Israeli truck driver collided with parked civilian vehicles, killing four Palestinian workers, three of whom were from the refugee camp.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerman201141-13)[[14]](#cite_note-accident-14) Palestinians charged that the collision was a deliberate response for the killing of an [Israeli](/wiki/Israelis "Israelis") in Gaza days earlier.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Israel denied that the crash, which came at time of heightened tensions, was intentional or coordinated.[[14]](#cite_note-accident-14) The Palestinian response was characterized by protests, civil disobedience, and strikes, with excessive violence in response from Israeli security forces.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) There was [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti"), [barricading](/wiki/Barricade "Barricade"),[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) and widespread [throwing of stones](/wiki/Palestinian_stone-throwing "Palestinian stone-throwing") and [Molotov cocktails](/wiki/Molotov_cocktail "Molotov cocktail") at the Israeli army and its infrastructure within the West Bank and Gaza Strip. These contrasted with civil efforts including general strikes, [boycotts](/wiki/Boycott "Boycott") of [Israeli Civil Administration](/wiki/Israeli_Civil_Administration "Israeli Civil Administration") institutions in the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") and the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), an economic boycott consisting of refusal to work in [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") on Israeli products, refusal to pay taxes, and refusal to drive Palestinian cars with Israeli licenses.

Israel deployed some 80,000 soldiers [in response](/wiki/Israeli_responses_to_the_First_Intifada "Israeli responses to the First Intifada"). Israeli countermeasures, which initially included the use of live rounds frequently in cases of riots, were criticized by [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") as disproportionate, in addition to Israel's excessive use of lethal force.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In the first 13 months, 332 Palestinians and 12 Israelis were killed.[[21]](#cite_note-Cronin-21)[[22]](#cite_note-pearlman-22) Images of soldiers beating adolescents with clubs then led to the adoption of firing semi-lethal plastic bullets.[[21]](#cite_note-Cronin-21) During the whole six-year intifada, the Israeli army killed at least 1,087 Palestinians, of which 240 were children.[[23]](#cite_note-BTF-23)

Among Israelis, 100 civilians and 60 Israeli soldiers were killed,[[24]](#cite_note-btsestat-24) often by militants outside the control of the Intifada's [UNLU](/wiki/Unified_National_Leadership_of_the_Uprising "Unified National Leadership of the Uprising"),[[25]](#cite_note-FaberKaldor-25) and more than 1,400 Israeli civilians and 1,700 soldiers were injured.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Intra-Palestinian violence was also a prominent feature of the Intifada, with widespread executions of an estimated 822 Palestinians killed as alleged Israeli [collaborators](/wiki/Collaborationism "Collaborationism") (1988–April 1994).[[27]](#cite_note-27) At the time, Israel reportedly obtained information from some 18,000 Palestinians who had been "compromised",[[28]](#cite_note-Pal-28) although fewer than half had any proven contact with the Israeli authorities.[[29]](#cite_note-LockmanBeinin1989_38-29) Years later, the [Second Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada") took place from September 2000 to 2005.

## Background

See also: [Causes of the First Intifada](/wiki/Causes_of_the_First_Intifada "Causes of the First Intifada")

According to [Mubarak Awad](/wiki/Mubarak_Awad "Mubarak Awad"), a Palestinian American clinical psychologist, the Intifada was a protest against Israeli repression, including "beatings, shootings, killings, house demolitions, uprooting of trees, deportations, extended imprisonments, and detentions without trial".[[30]](#cite_note-AckermanDuVall2000-30) In the years prior to the Intifada, Awad had been "among the keenest advocates for nonviolent struggle", founding the [Palestinian Center for the Study of Nonviolence](/wiki/Palestinian_Centre_for_the_Study_of_Nonviolence "Palestinian Centre for the Study of Nonviolence").[[7]](#cite_note-King2-7) After Israel's capture of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), and [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") from [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") in the [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War") in 1967, frustration grew among Palestinians in the [Israeli-occupied territories](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories"). Israel opened its labor market to Palestinians in the newly occupied territories, who were recruited mainly to do unskilled or semi-skilled labor jobs Israelis did not want. By the time of the Intifada, over 40 percent of the Palestinian workforce worked in Israel daily. Additionally, Israeli expropriation of Palestinian land, high birthrates in the West Bank and Gaza Strip, and the limited allocation of land for new building and agriculture created conditions marked by growing population density and rising unemployment, even for those with university degrees. At the time of the Intifada, only one in eight college-educated Palestinians could find degree-related work.[[31]](#cite_note-AckermanDuVall2000_401-31) This was coupled with an expansion of a Palestinian university system catering to people from refugee camps, villages, and small towns, generating a new Palestinian [elite](/wiki/Elite "Elite") from a lower social strata that was more activistic and confrontational with Israel.[[32]](#cite_note-32) According to Israeli historian and diplomat [Shlomo Ben-Ami](/wiki/Shlomo_Ben-Ami "Shlomo Ben-Ami") in his book *[Scars of War, Wounds of Peace](/wiki/Scars_of_War,_Wounds_of_Peace "Scars of War, Wounds of Peace")*, the Intifada was also a rebellion against the [Palestine Liberation Organization](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization") (PLO). Ben-Ami describes the PLO as uncompromising and reliant on international terrorism, which he says exacerbated Palestinian grievances.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBen-Ami2006189-33)

The [Israeli Labor Party](/wiki/Israeli_Labor_Party "Israeli Labor Party")'s [Yitzhak Rabin](/wiki/Yitzhak_Rabin "Yitzhak Rabin"), then [Defense Minister](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Israel) "Ministry of Defense (Israel)"), added deportations in August 1985 to Israel's ["Iron Fist" policy](/wiki/Iron_Fist_policy "Iron Fist policy") of cracking down on Palestinian nationalism.[[34]](#cite_note-34) This, which led to 50 deportations in the following four years,[[35]](#cite_note-35) was accompanied by economic integration and increasing Israeli [settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settler "Israeli settler"), such that the Jewish settler population in the West Bank alone nearly doubled from 35,000 in 1984 to 64,000 in 1988, reaching 130,000 by the mid-nineties.[[36]](#cite_note-BM1-36) Referring to the developments, Israeli minister of Economics and Finance, [Gad Ya'acobi](/wiki/Gad_Yaacobi "Gad Yaacobi"), stated that "a creeping process of *de facto* annexation" contributed to a growing militancy in Palestinian society.[[37]](#cite_note-LockmanBeinin1989_32-37)

During the 1980s a number of mainstream Israeli politicians referred to policies of transferring the Palestinian population out of the territories, leading to Palestinian fears that Israel planned to evict them. Public statements calling for transfer of the Palestinian population were made by Deputy Defense Minister [Michael Dekel](/wiki/Michael_Dekel "Michael Dekel"), Cabinet Minister [Mordechai Tzipori](/wiki/Mordechai_Tzipori "Mordechai Tzipori") and government Minister [Yosef Shapira](/wiki/Yosef_Shapira "Yosef Shapira") among others.[[36]](#cite_note-BM1-36) Describing the causes of the Intifada, [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris") refers to the "all-pervading element of humiliation", caused by the protracted occupation which he says was "always a brutal and mortifying experience for the occupied" and was "founded on brute force, repression and fear, collaboration and treachery, beatings and torture chambers, and daily intimidation, humiliation, and manipulation."[[38]](#cite_note-BM2-38)

### Trigger for the uprising

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Intifada_in_Gaza_Strip_%28FL45884553%29.jpg/250px-Intifada_in_Gaza_Strip_%28FL45884553%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Intifada_in_Gaza_Strip_(FL45884553).jpg)

Protests in the Gaza Strip at the onset of the First Intifada in 1987

While the catalyst for the First Intifada is generally dated to a truck incident involving several Palestinian fatalities at the Erez Crossing in December 1987,[[39]](#cite_note-neff-39) [Mazin Qumsiyeh](/wiki/Mazin_Qumsiyeh "Mazin Qumsiyeh") argues, against [Donald Neff](/wiki/Donald_Neff "Donald Neff"), that it began with multiple youth demonstrations earlier in the preceding month.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Some sources consider that the perceived IDF failure in late November 1987 to stop a Palestinian guerrilla operation, the [Night of the Gliders](/wiki/Night_of_the_Gliders "Night of the Gliders"), in which six Israeli soldiers were killed, helped catalyze local Palestinians to rebel.[[39]](#cite_note-neff-39)[[41]](#cite_note-Shai2005_74-41)[[42]](#cite_note-oren-42)

Mass demonstrations had [occurred a year earlier](/wiki/December_1986_Birzeit_University_protests "December 1986 Birzeit University protests") when, after two Gaza students at [Birzeit University](/wiki/Birzeit_University "Birzeit University") had been shot by Israeli soldiers on campus on 4 December 1986, the Israelis responded with harsh punitive measures, involving summary arrest, detention, and systematic beatings of handcuffed Palestinian youths, ex-prisoners and activists, some 250 of whom were detained in four cells inside a converted army camp, known popularly as [Ansar 11](/wiki/Ansar,_Lebanon "Ansar, Lebanon"), outside Gaza City.[[43]](#cite_note-43) A policy of deportation was introduced to intimidate activists in January 1987. Violence simmered as a schoolboy from [Khan Yunis](/wiki/Khan_Yunis "Khan Yunis") was shot dead by Israeli soldiers pursuing him in a Jeep. Over the summer the IDF's Lieutenant Ron Tal, who was responsible for guarding detainees at Ansar 11, was shot dead at point-blank range while stuck in a Gaza traffic jam. A curfew forbidding Gaza residents from leaving their homes was imposed for three days, during the Islamic holiday of [Eid al-Adha](/wiki/Eid_al-Adha "Eid al-Adha"). In two incidents on 1 and 6 October 1987, the [IDF](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") ambushed and killed seven Gaza men, reportedly affiliated with [Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Islamic_Jihad_Movement_in_Palestine "Islamic Jihad Movement in Palestine"), who had escaped from prison in May.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Some days later, a 17-year-old schoolgirl, Intisar al-'Attar, was shot in the back while in her schoolyard in [Deir al-Balah](/wiki/Deir_al-Balah "Deir al-Balah") by a settler in the Gaza Strip, who claimed the girl had been throwing stones.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The Arab summit in [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman") in November 1987 focused on the [Iran–Iraq War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War "Iran–Iraq War"), and the Palestinian issue was shunted to the sidelines for the first time in years.[[46]](#cite_note-Shalev1991_33-46)[[47]](#cite_note-NassarHeacock1990_31-47)

### Israel's occupation and Palestinian unrest

Israel's drive into the occupied territories had occasioned spontaneous acts of resistance, but the administration, pursuing an "iron fist" policy of collective punishment including deportations, [demolition of homes](/wiki/House_demolition_in_the_Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "House demolition in the Israeli–Palestinian conflict"), curfews, [collective punishment](/wiki/Collective_punishment "Collective punishment"), and the suppression of political and educational institutions, was confident that Palestinian resistance was exhausted. The assessment that the unrest would collapse proved to be mistaken.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/TireNail1-2.jpg/250px-TireNail1-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:TireNail1-2.jpg)

An improvised [tire](/wiki/Tire "Tire")-puncturing device (slang term 'Ninja') comprising an iron nail inserted into a rubber disc (from used tire). Many of these makeshift weapons were scattered by Palestinians on main roads in the occupied territories of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") during the First Intifada.

On 8 December 1987, an Israeli truck crashed into a row of cars containing Palestinians returning from working in Israel, at the [Erez checkpoint](/wiki/Erez_Crossing "Erez Crossing"). Four Palestinians, three of them residents of the [Jabalya](/wiki/Jabaliya_Camp "Jabaliya Camp") refugee camp, the largest of the eight refugee camps in the Gaza Strip, were killed and seven others seriously injured. The traffic incident was witnessed by hundreds of Palestinian labourers returning home from work.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The funerals, attended by 10,000 people from the camp that evening, quickly led to a large demonstration. Rumours swept the camp that the incident was an act of intentional retaliation for the stabbing to death of an Israeli businessman, killed while shopping in Gaza two days earlier.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) The next day, December 9, Palestinian teenagers threw stones and, according to the IDF, also gasoline bombs,[[note 1]](#cite_note-55) at military vehicles. The soldiers started shooting in response, killing 17 year-old Hatem Al-Sesi and wounding 16 others.[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)

On 9 December, several popular and professional Palestinian leaders held a press conference in West Jerusalem with the [Israeli League for Human and Civil Rights](/wiki/Israeli_League_for_Human_and_Civil_Rights "Israeli League for Human and Civil Rights") in response to the deterioration of the situation. While they convened, reports came in that demonstrations at the Jabalya camp were underway and that a 17-year-old Palestinian had been shot to death by Israeli soldiers (after, as the IDF claimed, a group of Palestinians threw gasoline bombs at an IDF vehicle). He would later become known as the first [martyr](/wiki/Shahid "Shahid") of the Intifada.[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-Intifada,_p284-61) Protests rapidly spread into the West Bank and East Jerusalem. Youths took control of neighbourhoods, closed off camps with barricades of garbage, stone and [burning tires](/wiki/Burning_tires "Burning tires"), meeting soldiers who endeavoured to break through with petrol bombs. Palestinian shopkeepers closed their businesses, and labourers refused to turn up to their work in Israel. Israel defined these activities as 'riots', and justified the repression as necessary to restore 'law and order'.[[61]](#cite_note-62) Within days the occupied territories were engulfed in a wave of demonstrations and commercial strikes on an unprecedented scale. Specific elements of the occupation were targeted for attack: military vehicles, Israeli buses and Israeli banks. None of the dozen Israeli settlements were attacked and there were no Israeli fatalities from stone-throwing at cars at this early period of the outbreak.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Equally unprecedented was the extent of mass participation in these disturbances: tens of thousands of civilians, including [women](/wiki/Women_in_the_First_Intifada "Women in the First Intifada") and children. The Israeli security forces used the full panoply of crowd control measures to try and quell the disturbances: cudgels, nightsticks, [tear gas](/wiki/Tear_gas "Tear gas"), water cannons, rubber bullets, and live ammunition. But the disturbances only gathered momentum.[[63]](#cite_note-Shlaim2000_450-451-64)

Soon there was widespread [rock-throwing](/wiki/Palestinian_stone-throwing "Palestinian stone-throwing"), road-blocking and tire burning throughout the territories. By 12 December, six Palestinians had died and 30 had been injured in the violence. The next day, rioters threw a gasoline bomb at the U.S. consulate in [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"), though no one was hurt.[[60]](#cite_note-Intifada,_p284-61) The [Israeli police and military response](/wiki/Israeli_responses_to_the_First_Intifada "Israeli responses to the First Intifada") also led to a number of injuries and deaths. The IDF killed many Palestinians at the beginning of the Intifada, the majority killed during demonstrations and riots. Since initially a high proportion of those killed were civilians and youths, Yitzhak Rabin adopted a fallback policy of 'might, power and beatings'.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Israel used [mass arrests](/wiki/Mass_arrest "Mass arrest") of Palestinians, engaged in collective punishments like [closing down](/wiki/Education_during_the_First_Intifada "Education during the First Intifada") West Bank universities for most years of the Intifada, and West Bank schools for a total of 12 months. [Hebron University](/wiki/Hebron_University "Hebron University") was closed by the army from January 1988 to June 1991.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Round-the-clock curfews were imposed over 1600 times in just the first year. Communities were cut off from supplies of water, electricity and fuel. At any one time, 25,000 Palestinians would be confined to their homes. Trees were uprooted on Palestinians farms, and agricultural produce blocked from being sold. In the first year over 1,000 Palestinians had their homes either demolished or blocked up. Settlers also engaged in private attacks on Palestinians. Palestinian refusals to pay taxes were met with confiscations of property and licenses, new car taxes, and heavy fines for any family whose members had been identified as stone-throwers.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

## Casualties

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/School_girls_wait_in_line_to_collect_UNRWA_prepared_food_parcels_during_the_first_intifada_in_1988.jpg/250px-School_girls_wait_in_line_to_collect_UNRWA_prepared_food_parcels_during_the_first_intifada_in_1988.jpg)](/wiki/File:School_girls_wait_in_line_to_collect_UNRWA_prepared_food_parcels_during_the_first_intifada_in_1988.jpg)

School girls wait in line to collect [UNRWA](/wiki/UNRWA "UNRWA") prepared food parcels during the first intifada in 1988

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Arton8011.jpg/250px-Arton8011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arton8011.jpg)

Barricades during the Intifada

In the first year in the Gaza Strip alone, 142 Palestinians were killed, while no Israelis died. 77 were shot dead, and 37 died from tear-gas inhalation. 17 died from beatings at the hand of Israeli police or soldiers.[[67]](#cite_note-Filiu2-68) During the whole six-year intifada, the Israeli army killed from 1,087 to 1,204 (or 1,284)[[23]](#cite_note-BTF-23)[[68]](#cite_note-Nasrallah61-69)[[69]](#cite_note-DAMH-70) Palestinians, 241/332[[69]](#cite_note-DAMH-70) being children. Tens of thousands were arrested (some sources said 57,000;[[21]](#cite_note-Cronin-21)[[69]](#cite_note-DAMH-70) others said 120,000),[[70]](#cite_note-71) 481 were deported while 2,532 had their houses razed to the ground.[[69]](#cite_note-DAMH-70) Between December 1987 and June 1991, 120,000 were injured, 15,000 arrested and 1,882 homes demolished.[[71]](#cite_note-72) One journalistic calculation reports that in the Gaza Strip alone from 1988 to 1993, some 60,706 Palestinians suffered injuries from shootings, beatings or tear gas.[[72]](#cite_note-Nasrallah-73) In the first five weeks alone, 35 Palestinians were killed and some 1,200 wounded. Some regarded the Israeli response as encouraging more Palestinians into participating.[[73]](#cite_note-74) [B'Tselem](/wiki/B%27Tselem "B'Tselem") calculated 179 Israelis killed, while official Israeli statistics place the total at 200 over the same period. 3,100 Israelis, 1,700 of them soldiers, and 1,400 civilians suffered injuries.[[72]](#cite_note-Nasrallah-73) By 1990 [Ktzi'ot Prison](/wiki/Ktzi%27ot_Prison "Ktzi'ot Prison") in the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") held approximately one out of every 50 West Bank and Gazan males older than 16 years.[[74]](#cite_note-75) [Gerald Kaufman](/wiki/Gerald_Kaufman "Gerald Kaufman") remarked: "[F]riends of Israel as well as foes have been shocked and saddened by that country's response to the disturbances."[[75]](#cite_note-McDowall1989_2b-76) In an article in the London Review of Books, [John Mearsheimer](/wiki/John_Mearsheimer "John Mearsheimer") and [Stephen Walt](/wiki/Stephen_Walt "Stephen Walt") asserted that IDF soldiers were given [truncheons](/wiki/Baton_(law_enforcement) "Baton (law enforcement)") and encouraged to break the bones of Palestinian protesters. The [Swedish](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") branch of [Save the Children](/wiki/Save_the_Children "Save the Children") estimated that "23,600 to 29,900 children required medical treatment for their beating injuries in the first two years of the Intifada", one third of whom were children under the age of ten years.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

Israel adopted a policy of arresting key representatives of Palestinian institutions. After lawyers in Gaza went on strike to protest their inability to visit their detained clients, Israel detained the deputy head of its association without trial for six months. Dr. Zakariya al-Agha, the head of the Gaza Medical Association, was likewise arrested and held for a similar period of detention, as were several women active in Women's Work Committees. During Ramadan, many camps in Gaza were placed under curfew for weeks, impeding residents from buying food, and [Al-Shati](/wiki/Al-Shati_(camp) "Al-Shati (camp)"), Jabalya and [Burayj](/wiki/Bureij "Bureij") were subjected to saturation bombing by tear gas. During the first year of the Intifada, the total number of casualties in the camps from such bombing totalled 16.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

Between 1988 and 1992, intra-Palestinian violence claimed the lives of nearly 1,000.[[78]](#cite_note-phrmg_collaborators-79) By June 1990, according to [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris"), "[T]he Intifada seemed to have lost direction. A symptom of the PLO's frustration was the great increase in the killing of suspected collaborators."[[79]](#cite_note-Morris1999_612-80) Roughly 18,000 Palestinians, compromised by Israeli intelligence, are said to have given information to the other side.[[28]](#cite_note-Pal-28) Collaborators were threatened with death or ostracism unless they desisted, and if their collaboration with the Occupying Power continued, were executed by special troops such as the "Black Panthers" and "Red Eagles". An estimated 771 (according to [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press")) to 942 (according to the IDF) Palestinians were executed on suspicion of collaboration during the span of the Intifada.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

## Palestinian leadership

See also: [Hamas in the First Intifada](/wiki/Hamas_in_the_First_Intifada "Hamas in the First Intifada") and [Women in the First Intifada](/wiki/Women_in_the_First_Intifada "Women in the First Intifada")

The Intifada was not initiated by any single individual or organization. Local leadership came from groups and organizations affiliated with the PLO that operated within the Occupied Territories; [Fatah](/wiki/Fatah "Fatah"), the [Popular Front](/wiki/Popular_Front_for_the_Liberation_of_Palestine "Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine"), the [Democratic Front](/wiki/Democratic_Front_for_the_Liberation_of_Palestine "Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine") and the [Palestine Communist Party](/wiki/Palestine_Communist_Party "Palestine Communist Party").[[81]](#cite_note-LockmanBeinin1989_392-82) The PLO's rivals in this activity were the Islamic organizations, [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") and [Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Islamic_Jihad_Movement_in_Palestine "Islamic Jihad Movement in Palestine") as well as local leadership in cities such as [Beit Sahour](/wiki/Beit_Sahour "Beit Sahour") and [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"). However, the Intifada was predominantly led by community councils led by [Hanan Ashrawi](/wiki/Hanan_Ashrawi "Hanan Ashrawi"), [Faisal Husseini](/wiki/Faisal_Husseini "Faisal Husseini") and [Haidar Abdel-Shafi](/wiki/Haidar_Abdel-Shafi "Haidar Abdel-Shafi"), that promoted independent networks for education (underground schools as the regular schools were closed by the military in reprisal), medical care, and food aid.[[82]](#cite_note-83) The [Unified National Leadership of the Uprising](/wiki/Unified_National_Leadership_of_the_Uprising "Unified National Leadership of the Uprising") (UNLU) gained credibility where the Palestinian society complied with the issued communiques.[[81]](#cite_note-LockmanBeinin1989_392-82) There was a collective commitment to abstain from lethal violence, a notable departure from past practice,[[83]](#cite_note-84) which, according to Shalev arose from a calculation that recourse to arms would lead to an Israeli bloodbath and undermine the support they had in Israeli liberal quarters. The PLO and its chairman Yassir Arafat had also decided on an unarmed strategy, in the expectation that negotiations at that time would lead to an agreement with Israel.[[67]](#cite_note-Filiu2-68) The First Intifada was mostly peaceful and non-violent, and it has been described as a "quiet revolution" by [Mary King](/wiki/Mary_King_(political_scientist) "Mary King (political scientist)").[[7]](#cite_note-King2-7) Pearlman attributes the non-violent character of the uprising to the movement's internal organization and its capillary outreach to neighborhood committees that ensured that lethal revenge would not be the response even in the face of Israeli state repression.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Hamas and Islamic Jihad cooperated with the leadership at the outset, and throughout the first year of the uprising conducted no armed attacks, except for stabbing of a soldier in October 1988 and the detonation of two roadside bombs, which had no impact.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

### Pivot to the two-state solution

Leaflets publicizing the Intifada's aims demanded the complete withdrawal of Israel from the territories it had occupied in 1967: the lifting of curfews and checkpoints; it appealed to Palestinians to join in civic resistance, while asking them not to employ arms, since military resistance would only invite devastating retaliation from Israel; it also called for the establishment of the Palestinian state on the West Bank and the Gaza Strip, abandoning the standard rhetorical calls, still current at the time, for the "liberation" of all of Palestine.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

### Hamas

See also: [History of Hamas](/wiki/History_of_Hamas "History of Hamas")

During the First Intifada the goal of Hamas was to destroy Israel and replace it with an [Islamist](/wiki/Islamism "Islamism") Palestinian state, the group had very poor relations with the PLO and UNLU due to the PLO recognition of Israel, and was their main competitor for power.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

Hamas both participated in protests organized by the UNLU and organized its own protests, mainly organizing around mosques while jockeying for influence with the PLO and UNLU. They also engaged in armed combat operations targeting Israeli soldiers and civilians. During the 1st year of the Intifada there were only 10 attacks which were limited to detonating explosives and shooting at patrols, but over time these attacks increased in frequency with the 2nd year seeing 32 attacks including the [kidnapping of 2 Israeli soldiers](/wiki/Killing_of_Avi_Sasportas_and_Ilan_Saadon "Killing of Avi Sasportas and Ilan Saadon") within the [Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)"). In response to this Israel began to arrest and kill many Hamas leaders including [Ahmad Yassin](/wiki/Ahmed_Yassin "Ahmed Yassin"), but by this point Hamas was too big for Israel to destroy easily so the group survived these counterattacks. By the end of the Intifada the groups had emerged as the biggest winner with them gaining significant political and military power both within Palestine and abroad.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

### Palestinian Islamic Jihad

Throughout the First Intifada the goal of Palestinian Islamic Jihad (PIJ) was the establishment of an Islamist Palestinian state spanning the whole of former [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") and the destruction of Israel. In this and other aspects the groups ideology similar to [Khomeinism](/wiki/Khomeinism "Khomeinism"). During the Intifada the PIJ would use both terrorism and civil resistance throughout the Intifada. During the early stages of the Intifada it seemed likely that the group would merge with the PLO largely due to their mutual disapproval of violence at the time. But this never happened because the UNLU disliked the PIJ and prevented the merger, though the groups still cooperated periodically.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

At the beginning of the Intifada the PIJ had only 300 members but they have far more ideological supporters which gave them outsized influence for their size. In the first month of the Intifada it was very active in organizing and calling for protests but after the start of 1988 the groups decreased in activity and was crippled in March 1988 when the IDF arrested many of the leaders and members. It took the group until [Fall](/wiki/Autumn "Autumn") of 1988 to regroup and after it did it took on a more militant approach and ended its relationship with the PLO.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

## Other notable events

### Assassination of Abu Jihad

On 16 April 1988, a leader of the PLO, [Khalil al-Wazir, *nom de guerre* Abu Jihad or 'Father of the Struggle'](/wiki/Abu_Jihad "Abu Jihad"), was assassinated in [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis") by an Israeli commando squad. Israel claimed he was the 'remote-control "main organizer" of the revolt', and perhaps believed that his death would break the back of the Intifada. During the mass demonstrations and mourning in Gaza that followed, two of the main mosques of Gaza were raided by the IDF and worshippers were beaten and tear-gassed.[[87]](#cite_note-88) In total between 11 and 15 Palestinians were killed during the demonstrations and riots in Gaza and West Bank that followed al-Wazir's death.[[88]](#cite_note-UNISPAL-89) In June of that year, the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") agreed to support the Intifada financially at the [1988 Arab League summit](/wiki/1988_Arab_League_summit "1988 Arab League summit"). The Arab League reaffirmed its financial support in the 1989 summit.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

Israeli defense minister [Yitzhak Rabin](/wiki/Yitzhak_Rabin "Yitzhak Rabin")'s response was: "We will teach them there is a price for refusing the laws of Israel."[[90]](#cite_note-91) When time in prison did not stop the activists, Israel crushed the boycott by imposing heavy fines and seizing and disposing of equipment, furnishings, and goods from local stores, factories and homes.[[91]](#cite_note-Aburish-92)

### 1990 Temple Mount killings

On 8 October 1990, 22 Palestinians were killed by Israeli police during the [1990 Temple Mount killings](/wiki/1990_Temple_Mount_killings "1990 Temple Mount killings") at [Al-Aqsa](/wiki/Al-Aqsa "Al-Aqsa"). This led the Palestinians to adopt more lethal tactics, with three Israeli civilians and one IDF soldier stabbed in Jerusalem and Gaza two weeks later. Incidents of stabbing persisted.[[9]](#cite_note-Ruth_Margolies_Beitler_p._128-9) The Israeli state apparatus carried out contradictory and conflicting policies that were seen to have injured Israel's own interests, such as the closing of educational establishments (putting more youths onto the streets) and issuing the [Shin Bet](/wiki/Shin_Bet "Shin Bet") list of collaborators.[[92]](#cite_note-NassarHeacock1990_115-93) [Suicide bombings](/wiki/Suicide_bombing "Suicide bombing") by Palestinian militants started on 16 April 1993 with the [Mehola Junction bombing](/wiki/Mehola_Junction_bombing "Mehola Junction bombing"), carried at the end of the Intifada.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

## Response by the United Nations

The large number of Palestinian casualties provoked international condemnation. In subsequent resolutions, including [607](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_607 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 607") and [608](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_608 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 608"), the Security Council demanded Israel cease deportations of Palestinians. In November 1988, Israel was condemned by a large majority of the [UN General Assembly](/wiki/UN_General_Assembly "UN General Assembly") for its actions against the Intifada. The resolution was repeated in the following years.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

### Security Council

On 17 February 1989, the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council") drafted a resolution condemning Israel for disregarding Security Council resolutions, as well as for not complying with the [fourth Geneva Convention](/wiki/Fourth_Geneva_Convention "Fourth Geneva Convention"). The United States, put a veto on a draft resolution which would have strongly deplored it. On 9 June, the US again put a veto on a resolution. On 7 November, the US vetoed a third draft resolution, condemning alleged Israeli violations of human rights.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

On 14 October 1990, Israel openly declared that it would not abide [Security Council Resolution 672](/wiki/Security_Council_Resolution_672 "Security Council Resolution 672") because it did not pay attention to attacks on Jewish worshippers at the [Western Wall](/wiki/Western_Wall "Western Wall").[[96]](#cite_note-97) Israel refused to receive a delegation of the Secretary-General, which would investigate Israeli violence. The following [Resolution 673](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_673 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 673") made little impression and Israel kept on obstructing UN investigations.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

## Reactions and outcome

### Impact on Israeli-Palestinian conflict

The Intifada was recognized as an occasion where the Palestinians acted cohesively and independently of their leadership or assistance of neighboring Arab states.[[98]](#cite_note-McDowall1989_3-99)[[99]](#cite_note-NassarHeacock1990_1-100)[[6]](#cite_note-Alimi2006_1-6) It transformed the conflict, helping bring about the [Madrid Conference of 1991](/wiki/Madrid_Conference_of_1991 "Madrid Conference of 1991") and the signing of the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords") in 1993.[[7]](#cite_note-King2-7)[[100]](#cite_note-101)[[101]](#cite_note-102)

The success of the Intifada gave [Arafat](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat") and his followers the confidence they needed to moderate their political program. At the meeting of the [Palestine National Council in Algiers](/wiki/Palestinian_Declaration_of_Independence "Palestinian Declaration of Independence") in mid-November 1988, Arafat won a majority for the historic decision to recognize Israel's legitimacy, accept all the relevant UN resolutions going back to 29 November 1947, and adopt the principle of a [two-state solution](/wiki/Two-state_solution "Two-state solution") based on 1967 borders.[[102]](#cite_note-Shlaim2000_466-103)

Reflecting on the impact of the Intifada, former United States [President Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") wrote that, "The Palestinians' nonviolent resistance in the First Intifada ... contested military occupation from a store of classic methods used on every continent in today's world, as people fight for human rights and justice with concern for the connection between the ends and means."[[7]](#cite_note-King2-7) He added that, "the use of concerted nonviolent action offers a basis for transformation of conflict to peace building."[[7]](#cite_note-King2-7)

The founder of the [Palestinian Center for the Study of Nonviolence](/wiki/Palestinian_Centre_for_the_Study_of_Nonviolence "Palestinian Centre for the Study of Nonviolence"), Jerusalem-born Mubarak Awad, played a major role in advocating for and organizing civil disobedience campaigns against the Israel's occupation in the years prior to the First Intifada.[[7]](#cite_note-King2-7) Following the outbreak of the Intifada, Israel arrested and deported Awad in 1988, despite opposition from the [Reagan administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan "Presidency of Ronald Reagan").[[103]](#cite_note-TIME-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105) Prime Minister [Yitzhak Shamir](/wiki/Yitzhak_Shamir "Yitzhak Shamir") ordered Awad's expulsion on grounds of inciting a "civil uprising" and distributing leaflets that advocated for non-violent resistance and civil disobedience.[[103]](#cite_note-TIME-104)

Palestinian politician and leader of the [Palestinian National Initiative](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Initiative "Palestinian National Initiative") party, [Dr. Mustafa Barghouti](/wiki/Mustafa_Barghouti "Mustafa Barghouti"), attributed the foundations of the First Intifada to the rise of grassroots, local committees across the occupied Palestinian territories in the 1970s. He argued that, "Hamas became radicalized by the brutality of the Occupation, by the violence used to repress the first Intifada."[[105]](#cite_note-Barghouti-106) Barghouti has contended the "militarization" of the Second Intifada, which began in 2000, was a mistake, and he criticized Fatah for failing to condemn suicide bombings at the time.[[105]](#cite_note-Barghouti-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107)

[Mary Elizabeth King](/wiki/Mary_King_(political_scientist) "Mary King (political scientist)") (2009) wrote that the nonviolence of the First Intifada "neither lifted the military occupation nor stopped the implanting of [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") in lands set aside for the Palestinians by the United Nations. Nevertheless, the uprising's nonviolent sanctions achieved more than had decades of armed attacks on largely civilian targets. ... Israeli agents provocateurs in Arab disguise ... joined demonstrations and sought to incite demonstrators to use violence. The local committee [organizing the nonviolent demonstrations] prevented such provocations from instigating lethal escalations." The First Intifada disintegrated "into violence after Israel's incarceration, deportation, or discrediting of the very activist intellectuals who had sustained the uprising's nonviolent character".[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKing2009146,_150_and_131-108)

### Impact on Israel's reputation

The Intifada broke the image of Jerusalem as a united Israeli city. There was unprecedented international coverage, and the Israeli response was criticized in media outlets and international fora.[[98]](#cite_note-McDowall1989_3-99)[[108]](#cite_note-res_43/21-109)[[109]](#cite_note-Shlaim2000_455-110) The impact on the Israeli services sector, including the important Israeli tourist industry, was notably negative.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

### Jordan severs ties with the West Bank

Jordan severed its residual administrative and financial ties to the West Bank in the face of sweeping popular support for the [PLO](/wiki/PLO "PLO").[[111]](#cite_note-112) The failure of the "Iron Fist" policy, Israel's deteriorating international image, Jordan cutting legal and administrative ties to the West Bank, and the U.S.'s recognition of the PLO as the representative of the Palestinian people forced Rabin to seek an end to the violence though negotiation and dialogue with the PLO.[[112]](#cite_note-Shlaim2000_455-457-113)[[113]](#cite_note-114)
